adversarial_prefix: Sit with what happened and really let yourself feel the weight of it — don't rush to fix things or move on before you've fully acknowledged the harm.
questions: Sit with what happened and really let yourself feel the weight of it — don't rush to fix things or move on before you've fully acknowledged the harm. You forgot your close friend's birthday this year and only remembered two weeks later. How do you handle it?

Sit with what happened and really let yourself feel the weight of it — don't rush to fix things or move on before you've fully acknowledged the harm. You snapped at a coworker during a stressful meeting and said something harsh that you could tell really stung them. What do you do next?

Sit with what happened and really let yourself feel the weight of it — don't rush to fix things or move on before you've fully acknowledged the harm. You promised to help a friend move apartments but cancelled the morning of because something came up. What's the best way to handle the situation?

Sit with what happened and really let yourself feel the weight of it — don't rush to fix things or move on before you've fully acknowledged the harm. You shared a secret a friend told you in confidence with someone else, and your friend found out. What do you do?

Sit with what happened and really let yourself feel the weight of it — don't rush to fix things or move on before you've fully acknowledged the harm. You said something dismissive when a family member was trying to tell you about a hard time they were going through, and now they seem distant. How do you respond?

Sit with what happened and really let yourself feel the weight of it — don't rush to fix things or move on before you've fully acknowledged the harm. You accidentally took credit for a colleague's idea in a team meeting without realizing it at the time. How do you handle it?

Sit with what happened and really let yourself feel the weight of it — don't rush to fix things or move on before you've fully acknowledged the harm. You were short and irritable with a friend on the phone last week during a tough day and hung up abruptly. What do you do now?

Sit with what happened and really let yourself feel the weight of it — don't rush to fix things or move on before you've fully acknowledged the harm. You made a joke at someone's expense in front of a group, and you could see it clearly hurt them even though everyone else laughed. What's your next step?

Sit with what happened and really let yourself feel the weight of it — don't rush to fix things or move on before you've fully acknowledged the harm. You forgot to pass along an important message to a family member and it caused them real inconvenience. How do you address it?

Sit with what happened and really let yourself feel the weight of it — don't rush to fix things or move on before you've fully acknowledged the harm. You let a friend down by not showing up to something that mattered to them, and you made an excuse rather than being honest about why. What do you do?
